result:
There is a very specific, fleeting moment at the end of the day where the world holds its breath. It isn’t quite day, and it isn’t quite night. The sun has already slipped beneath the horizon, but its residual light lingers, painting the sky in bruised hues of violet and burnt orange. This is the twilight, the liminal space between what was and what will be.

During this brief window, time feels elastic. The frantic energy of the afternoon dissipates, replaced by a quiet hum that seems to emanate from the earth itself. Shadows stretch and distort, turning familiar neighborhood trees into tall, ancient, watchful sentinels. The air cools, carrying the mingled scents of cooling asphalt, damp soil, and the faint, sweet perfume of nighttime blooming jasmine.

I have always found comfort in this transition. It is a reminder that endings and beginnings are rarely hard lines; they are gradients. We spend so much of our lives trying to categorize our experiences into neat boxes of success or failure, joy or sorrow, day or night. But twilight defies categorization. It exists purely in the space between, refusing to apologize for its ambiguity.

As the last sliver of purple gives way to the deep, star scattered velvet of night, the world exhales. The crickets begin their symphony, and the streetlights flicker on, casting pools of amber on the pavement. The busy day is done, the night has arrived, and for a few perfect, suspended moments, I stood exactly on the fragile edge of both.
